signals.headline: **Consumer inflation expectations** jumped sharply: short-term expectations rose to 4
signals.raw:
**Consumer inflation expectations** jumped sharply: short-term expectations rose to 4.8% in the University of Michigan survey, while financial market-implied 2026 headline CPI inflation expectations rose 0.8 percentage points to 3.3%.[1]

These developments would likely impact Fed rate-cut expectations, energy-related investments, and inflation-linked securities.

signals.headline: # Recent Inflation Data and Announcements (April 2026)
matched_markets: Will annual inflation increase by 3.8% in April?

signals.headline: HEADLINE: March 2026 CPI Shows Sharp Inflation Spike Driven by Iran War Energy Surge**
signals.raw: HEADLINE: March 2026 CPI Shows Sharp Inflation Spike Driven by Iran War Energy Surge**

signals.headline: YES** – This would move prediction markets
signals.raw: YES** – This would move prediction markets

signals.headline: HIGH confidence**
signals.raw:
HIGH confidence**

The **headline CPI rose 3.29% year-over-year in March 2026**, with a particularly sharp monthly gain of **0.87%**, primarily driven by a **10.9% surge in energy prices** tied to the Iran war.[1][2] Gasoline prices spiked **21.2% in March**, accounting for nearly three-quarters of the monthly increase.[2] Oil prices jumped from an average of $71/barrel in February to $103/barrel

signals.headline: **Headline PCE inflation** reached **3
signals.raw: **Headline PCE inflation** reached **3.45% year-over-year**, with a monthly rise of 0.64%.[1]

signals.headline: **Core CPI remained more benign** at **2
signals.raw: **Core CPI remained more benign** at **2.6% year-over-year**, with only a 0.2% monthly increase, suggesting underlying inflation pressures are more contained.[2]

signals.headline: Goldman Sachs raised its December 2026 headline PCE inflation forecast by a full percentage point since the war began, w
signals.raw: Goldman Sachs raised its December 2026 headline PCE inflation forecast by a full percentage point since the war began, with energy accounting for most of the revision.[1]
